steps.title: Determine what word form the parenthetical position requires
steps.content:
The blank appears between two commas: "the researchers, ______ computer imaging to compare..., aimed to shed light." The main subject is "the researchers" and the main verb is "aimed." The bracketed phrase must function as a parenthetical modifier — describing how the researchers worked — without creating a second main verb. Eliminate any form that conflicts with this structure.
  • Choice A — 'to use': An infinitive phrase can modify a noun, but "to use computer imaging to compare" reads as a purpose phrase, not a description of concurrent action, and clashes with the surrounding syntax. ✗
  • Choice B — 'using': A present participle phrase set off by commas functions as a parenthetical modifier describing the researchers' method. Grammatically well-formed in Standard English. ✓
  • Choice C — 'used': A past participle here would imply the researchers were acted upon ("computer imaging" would need to be using them), which reverses the intended meaning. ✗
  • Choice D — 'use': A bare verb between commas would create a second finite verb competing with "aimed," producing a run-on sentence. ✗

steps.title: Apply the participial modifier rule
steps.content: Only a present participle can open a parenthetical modifier that describes how the subject performed the main action. Every other form either reverses the meaning, creates a structural error, or produces a purpose phrase that does not fit. That matches choice B — B is correct.
